---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log (каротажная диаграмма)** — a composite well log from a Soviet-era drilling and geophysical logging operation. It appears to be a standard Soviet-format stratigraphic and geophysical borehole log, presented on a tall narrow sheet typical of Soviet exploration reporting practice. The log includes multiple geophysical curve tracks plotted against depth, alongside a lithological column and descriptive annotations.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The log covers a single borehole (скважина), though the specific borehole number is **[illegible]** at this scan resolution. The header (верхняя часть) contains the standard Soviet borehole log title block — likely reading **"БУРОВИЙ ЖУРНАЛ"** or **"КАРОТАЖНАЯ ДИАГРАММА"** — associated with the regional survey of northern Kazakhstan (map sheets M-42 / N-42). The depth range covered appears to be substantial — estimated at **several hundred metres** based on the length of the log sheet — though exact depth values on the depth scale are **[illegible]**. The survey area is northern Kazakhstan as per the report context (Smolianova et al., 1983–1984).

---

## Key Content

The composite log displays the following visible track structure (left to right):

1. **Leftmost columns (narrow):** Appear to contain **depth scale markings** and possibly interval/core recovery notations. Column structure suggests metre-by-metre or 10-metre interval graduation.

2. **Lithological column:** A narrow hatched/patterned column representing **rock types encountered during drilling**. Different fill patterns (cross-hatching, dotted, blank) are visible at various depth intervals, indicating changes in lithology — likely alternating between sedimentary and/or crystalline basement units. Several distinct lithological boundaries are visible.

3. **Geophysical curve tracks (central portion — the most visually prominent part of the log):** Two or more geophysical curves are plotted, showing:
   - **High-amplitude, irregular fluctuations** in the upper-to-middle section of the log, consistent with **electrical resistivity (КС — кажущееся сопротивление)** or **spontaneous potential (ПС — потенциал самополяризации)** logs.
   - A **broad, darker-shaded zone** in the upper-middle portion of the log may represent a **filled/shaded resistivity curve** or a zone of particular geophysical significance (e.g., anomalous formation).
   - The curves show notably **high variability** (sharp deflections) in certain intervals, suggesting lithological heterogeneity or fractured/mineralised zones.
   - In the lower portion of the log, curves appear to become **lower amplitude and more regular**, possibly indicating more homogeneous basement rock.

4. **Right-hand columns:** Appear to contain **additional geophysical parameters** or **core description/annotation columns**, with some tabular data visible but **[illegible]** at this resolution.

5. **Lower section of the log:** A separate, shorter geophysical curve panel is visible at the very bottom of the sheet, possibly representing a **different logging run or a gamma-ray / neutron log** section.

6. **Legend/key block:** A small table or legend box is visible in the upper-right and lower-right corners — content is **[illegible]** but consistent with Soviet standard borehole log legends (lithology symbols, scale factors, equipment specifications).

No colour is present — the document is **black and white** (monochrome), as is standard for Soviet-era borehole log reproductions.

## Research Value

This composite borehole log is a primary subsurface data source that directly documents the **lithological succession and petrophysical properties** encountered at a specific drill site in the northern Kazakhstan survey area, providing ground-truth data to correlate with the regional seismic, gravity, and magnetic surveys described in Report 36572. The geophysical curves (likely SP, resistivity, and possibly gamma-ray) allow identification of **formation boundaries, mineralised intervals, and anomalous zones** that may indicate economic mineral potential (e.g., ore-bearing horizons or favourable structural traps). For an AI research assistant, this log would be essential for **depth-calibrating geophysical interpretations** and constraining the subsurface geological model of the area, particularly in identifying basement depth and cover sequence properties relevant to mineral exploration targeting.
